# The Oculus — API Contract

Status: **Placeholder — Pre-Implementation**

Package: `@shardworks/oculus-apparatus` · Plugin id: `oculus`

> **⚠️ Pre-implementation.** This document captures the design decisions for the Oculus. It will be expanded into a full API contract during implementation.

---

## Purpose

The Oculus is the guild's web dashboard. It serves a browser-based interface for observing and interacting with guild state, providing a persistent visual overview that the CLI and MCP tool server do not offer.

The Oculus is an **aggregator, not a producer** — it does not own any guild data. It consumes `pages` and `routes` from kit contributions, auto-maps installed guild tools as REST endpoints, and serves a unified multi-page UI with shared chrome and styling. Any plugin can extend the dashboard by contributing a page.

The Oculus does **not** handle authentication, authorization beyond tool-level permissions, server-side rendering, or asset bundling.

---

## Dependencies

```
requires: ['tools']      — needs the Instrumentarium for tool resolution and auto-mapping
consumes: ['pages', 'routes']
```

- **The Instrumentarium** — the Oculus resolves tools callable by `'web'` and auto-maps them as REST endpoints.

---

## Kit Interface

Kits and apparatus contribute pages and custom API routes to the Oculus. A page is a directory of static assets (HTML, CSS, JS) with an `index.html` entry point. Custom routes handle patterns that don't fit the tool model (SSE streams, WebSocket connections, etc.).

```typescript
// Example: an apparatus contributing a page via supportKit
export default {
  apparatus: {
    // ...
    supportKit: {
      pages: [{
        id: 'writs',
        title: 'Writs',
        assetsDir: path.join(__dirname, '../assets/writs'),
      }],
      routes: [{
        method: 'GET',
        path: '/api/session/:id/stream',
        handler: sseHandler,
      }],
    },
  },
} satisfies Plugin
```

---

## Support Kit

The Oculus has no support kit — it does not contribute tools, engines, or relays.

---

## `OculusApi` Interface (`provides`)

```typescript
interface OculusApi {
  /** The port the server is listening on. */
  port(): number
  /** The full base URL (e.g. http://localhost:7470). */
  url(): string
  /** List of registered pages with id, title, and URL. */
  pages(): PageInfo[]
}
```

---

## Configuration

```json
{
  "oculus": {
    "port": 7470
  }
}
```

**`port`** — the HTTP port for the dashboard server. Default: `7470`.

---

## Key Design Decisions

These decisions were made during patron design review and are **authoritative constraints**, not suggestions.

### Web Server: Hono

The Oculus uses [Hono](https://hono.dev/) as its HTTP framework. Hono has zero transitive dependencies, built-in TypeScript, and a clean async API. It was selected over Express (65 transitive deps), Fastify (42 deps, competing plugin model), and Koa (30 deps + separate router package). Hono is a framework dependency of the Oculus package — not exposed to page authors.

### Pages, Not Widgets

The Oculus provides full-page contributions only — no widgets, cards, or dashboard fragments. Each page is a self-contained view. There is no cross-plugin layout coordination, no widget grid, no shared dashboard surface. The Oculus provides a home page that lists all registered pages with links.

### Static Asset Directories

Each page contribution points to a directory of static files with an `index.html` entry point. The Oculus serves these files under `/pages/{id}/`. No server-side rendering, no templating, no build step imposed by the framework. Plugins may use their own build steps in their publish pipelines — the Oculus just serves the output directory.

Page assets are served from disk on each request (no caching) for hot-reload during development.

### Tool→REST Auto-Mapping with CLI-Mirror Naming

Guild tools are automatically exposed as REST endpoints. The URL convention mirrors the CLI's hyphen-prefix grouping — a mechanical transform with no ambiguity:

```
writ-list       → GET  /api/writ/list
commission-post → POST /api/commission/post
rig-for-writ    → GET  /api/rig/for-writ
signal          → POST /api/signal
```

First hyphen splits prefix from rest, prepend `/api/`. No RESTful resource inference, no pluralization.

HTTP method is inferred from the tool's `permission` level: `read` (or no permission) → GET, `write`/`delete`/`admin` → POST.

For GET endpoints, query string parameters require string→number/boolean coercion (same pattern as the CLI's `coerceCliOpts`).

### `'web'` Caller Type

`ToolCaller` in `@shardworks/tools-apparatus` is extended to include `'web'`: `'cli' | 'anima' | 'library' | 'web'`. Tools with no `callableBy` restriction remain available to all callers including web. Tools that explicitly restrict `callableBy` must include `'web'` to be auto-mapped. This is the only change to existing framework code outside the Oculus package.

### Custom Routes for Escape Hatches

For patterns that don't fit the tool model (SSE streams, WebSocket, long-polling), plugins contribute explicit route definitions with a method, path, and Hono handler. Custom routes must be under `/api/`. If a custom route conflicts with an auto-mapped tool route, the custom route wins with a startup warning.

### Chrome Injection (Server-Side)

The Oculus intercepts page `index.html` responses and injects: (1) a stylesheet link in `<head>`, and (2) a navigation header after `<body>`. Page authors get navigation and styling with zero boilerplate. Pages without recognizable `<head>` or `<body>` tags are served as-is.

### Shared Stylesheet: Three Layers

The Oculus ships a static CSS file at `/oculus/styles.css` providing:

1. **CSS custom properties** for theming — `--bg`, `--surface`, `--border`, `--text`, color accents
2. **Element-type selectors** for baseline — `body`, `table`, `button`, `input`, `pre`, `code`, headings, links all styled with no classes needed
3. **Utility classes** for common patterns — `.card`, `.badge`, `.badge--success`, `.data-table`, `.btn`, `.toolbar`, `.empty-state`

### Self-Documenting API Index

`GET /api/_tools` returns a JSON listing of all auto-mapped tools with their route, HTTP method, and parameter schemas.

---

## Open Questions

- **Permission model for the web surface.** Currently no auth — the dashboard is localhost-only. If remote access is ever needed, what's the permission model? Deferred.
- **Client-side routing.** Pages are currently full page loads. If SPAs want hash or history routing within their page, does the Oculus need to support fallback routes per page? Probably not for MVP.

---

## Future: Component Library

The shared stylesheet covers the baseline. As patterns emerge across multiple pages (sortable tables, modal dialogs, status badges with live updates), a small JS component library (Web Components or plain helper functions) may be extracted. This is explicitly deferred — build it when we see what repeats.

# The Oculus — API Contract

Status: **Placeholder — Pre-Implementation**

Package: `@shardworks/oculus-apparatus` · Plugin id: `oculus`

> **⚠️ Pre-implementation.** This document captures the design decisions for the Oculus. It will be expanded into a full API contract during implementation.

---

## Purpose

The Oculus is the guild's web dashboard. It serves a browser-based interface for observing and interacting with guild state, providing a persistent visual overview that the CLI and MCP tool server do not offer.

The Oculus is an **aggregator, not a producer** — it does not own any guild data. It consumes `pages` and `routes` from kit contributions, auto-maps installed guild tools as REST endpoints, and serves a unified multi-page UI with shared chrome and styling. Any plugin can extend the dashboard by contributing a page.

The Oculus does **not** handle authentication, authorization beyond tool-level permissions, server-side rendering, or asset bundling.

---

## Dependencies

```
requires: ['tools']      — needs the Instrumentarium for tool resolution and auto-mapping
consumes: ['pages', 'routes']
```

- **The Instrumentarium** — the Oculus resolves tools callable by `'web'` and auto-maps them as REST endpoints.

---

## Kit Interface

Kits and apparatus contribute pages and custom API routes to the Oculus. A page is a directory of static assets (HTML, CSS, JS) with an `index.html` entry point. Custom routes handle patterns that don't fit the tool model (SSE streams, WebSocket connections, etc.).

```typescript
// Example: an apparatus contributing a page via supportKit
export default {
  apparatus: {
    // ...
    supportKit: {
      pages: [{
        id: 'writs',
        title: 'Writs',
        assetsDir: path.join(__dirname, '../assets/writs'),
      }],
      routes: [{
        method: 'GET',
        path: '/api/session/:id/stream',
        handler: sseHandler,
      }],
    },
  },
} satisfies Plugin
```

---

## Support Kit

The Oculus has no support kit — it does not contribute tools, engines, or relays.

---

## `OculusApi` Interface (`provides`)

```typescript
interface OculusApi {
  /** The port the server is listening on. */
  port(): number
  /** The full base URL (e.g. http://localhost:7470). */
  url(): string
  /** List of registered pages with id, title, and URL. */
  pages(): PageInfo[]
}
```

---

## Configuration

```json
{
  "oculus": {
    "port": 7470
  }
}
```

**`port`** — the HTTP port for the dashboard server. Default: `7470`.

---

## Key Design Decisions

These decisions were made during patron design review and are **authoritative constraints**, not suggestions.

### Web Server: Hono

The Oculus uses [Hono](https://hono.dev/) as its HTTP framework. Hono has zero transitive dependencies, built-in TypeScript, and a clean async API. It was selected over Express (65 transitive deps), Fastify (42 deps, competing plugin model), and Koa (30 deps + separate router package). Hono is a framework dependency of the Oculus package — not exposed to page authors.

### Pages, Not Widgets

The Oculus provides full-page contributions only — no widgets, cards, or dashboard fragments. Each page is a self-contained view. There is no cross-plugin layout coordination, no widget grid, no shared dashboard surface. The Oculus provides a home page that lists all registered pages with links.

### Static Asset Directories

Each page contribution points to a directory of static files with an `index.html` entry point. The Oculus serves these files under `/pages/{id}/`. No server-side rendering, no templating, no build step imposed by the framework. Plugins may use their own build steps in their publish pipelines — the Oculus just serves the output directory.

Page assets are served from disk on each request (no caching) for hot-reload during development.

### Tool→REST Auto-Mapping with CLI-Mirror Naming

Guild tools are automatically exposed as REST endpoints. The URL convention mirrors the CLI's hyphen-prefix grouping — a mechanical transform with no ambiguity:

```
writ-list       → GET  /api/writ/list
commission-post → POST /api/commission/post
rig-for-writ    → GET  /api/rig/for-writ
signal          → POST /api/signal
```

First hyphen splits prefix from rest, prepend `/api/`. No RESTful resource inference, no pluralization.

HTTP method is inferred from the tool's `permission` level: `read` (or no permission) → GET, `write`/`delete`/`admin` → POST.

For GET endpoints, query string parameters require string→number/boolean coercion (same pattern as the CLI's `coerceCliOpts`).

### `'web'` Caller Type

`ToolCaller` in `@shardworks/tools-apparatus` is extended to include `'web'`: `'cli' | 'anima' | 'library' | 'web'`. Tools with no `callableBy` restriction remain available to all callers including web. Tools that explicitly restrict `callableBy` must include `'web'` to be auto-mapped. This is the only change to existing framework code outside the Oculus package.

### Custom Routes for Escape Hatches

For patterns that don't fit the tool model (SSE streams, WebSocket, long-polling), plugins contribute explicit route definitions with a method, path, and Hono handler. Custom routes must be under `/api/`. If a custom route conflicts with an auto-mapped tool route, the custom route wins with a startup warning.

### Chrome Injection (Server-Side)

The Oculus intercepts page `index.html` responses and injects: (1) a stylesheet link in `<head>`, and (2) a navigation header after `<body>`. Page authors get navigation and styling with zero boilerplate. Pages without recognizable `<head>` or `<body>` tags are served as-is.

### Shared Stylesheet: Three Layers

The Oculus ships a static CSS file at `/oculus/styles.css` providing:

1. **CSS custom properties** for theming — `--bg`, `--surface`, `--border`, `--text`, color accents
2. **Element-type selectors** for baseline — `body`, `table`, `button`, `input`, `pre`, `code`, headings, links all styled with no classes needed
3. **Utility classes** for common patterns — `.card`, `.badge`, `.badge--success`, `.data-table`, `.btn`, `.toolbar`, `.empty-state`

### Self-Documenting API Index

`GET /api/_tools` returns a JSON listing of all auto-mapped tools with their route, HTTP method, and parameter schemas.

---

## Open Questions

- **Permission model for the web surface.** Currently no auth — the dashboard is localhost-only. If remote access is ever needed, what's the permission model? Deferred.
- **Client-side routing.** Pages are currently full page loads. If SPAs want hash or history routing within their page, does the Oculus need to support fallback routes per page? Probably not for MVP.

---

## Future: Component Library

The shared stylesheet covers the baseline. As patterns emerge across multiple pages (sortable tables, modal dialogs, status badges with live updates), a small JS component library (Web Components or plain helper functions) may be extracted. This is explicitly deferred — build it when we see what repeats.

Nope, no good reason — I was being lazy and collapsing everything that isn't a read into POST. You're right, `delete` → DELETE is the obvious mapping and we should use it.

Updated mental model:

| Permission level | HTTP method |
|---|---|
| `read` (or none) | GET |
| `write` | POST |
| `delete` | DELETE |
| `admin` | POST |

`admin` stays POST since admin operations are usually "do a thing" (restart, vacuum, migrate) rather than "remove a thing."

# The Oculus — Web Dashboard Apparatus — Commission Brief

## Problem

The guild has two interface surfaces: the `nsg` CLI and the MCP tool server (for anima sessions). Neither provides a persistent, visual overview of guild state. Previous attempts at a web dashboard produced a single monolithic apparatus and produced a broken 1000-line inline-HTML file — no extensibility, no separation of concerns, fatal JS syntax errors from escaped quotes in template literals.

What's missing is a framework-level web dashboard apparatus that any plugin can extend with its own pages and API endpoints, following the same kit contribution pattern used by tools, engines, and relays.

## Goal

A new apparatus ("The Oculus") that serves a web dashboard for the guild. Plugins contribute pages as static asset directories. Guild tools are automatically exposed as REST endpoints. The Oculus provides shared chrome (navigation) and a base stylesheet so contributed pages look cohesive with zero effort.

## Design Decisions

These decisions were made during design and should be treated as constraints, not suggestions.

### Web server: Hono

Use Hono as the HTTP framework. It has zero transitive dependencies (vs 65 for Express, 42 for Fastify, 30 for Koa), built-in TypeScript support, and a clean async API. It's a framework dependency of the Oculus package — not exposed to page authors.

### Pages as kit contributions, not widgets

The Oculus `consumes: ['pages', 'routes']`. Plugins contribute full pages — not widgets, cards, or dashboard fragments. Each page is a self-contained view. There is no cross-plugin layout coordination, no widget grid, no shared dashboard surface. If an overview/home page is wanted, the Oculus itself provides one (listing registered pages with links).

Pages may also be contributed through apparatus `supportKit`, following the same pattern as tools and engines.

### Pages are static asset directories

Each page contribution points to a directory containing static files (HTML, CSS, JS) with an `index.html` entry point. The Oculus serves these files under `/pages/{id}/`. No server-side rendering, no templating engine, no build step imposed by the Oculus. Plugins *may* use their own build steps (e.g., bundling a React app) in their publish pipeline, but that's the plugin's concern — the Oculus just serves the output directory.

Page assets are served from disk on each request (no caching). This gives hot-reload during development for free.

### Tool→REST auto-mapping with CLI-mirror naming

The Oculus automatically exposes guild tools as REST endpoints. The URL convention mirrors the CLI's hyphen-prefix grouping:

- `writ-list` → `GET /api/writ/list`
- `commission-post` → `POST /api/commission/post`
- `rig-for-writ` → `GET /api/rig/for-writ`
- `signal` → `POST /api/signal`

This is a mechanical transform — first hyphen splits prefix from rest, prepend `/api/`. No RESTful resource inference, no pluralization, no ambiguity.

HTTP method is inferred from the tool's `permission` level: `read` (or no permission) → GET, `write`/`delete`/`admin` → POST.

For GET endpoints, query string parameters need the same string→number/boolean coercion the CLI applies (all query params arrive as strings; the tool's Zod schema expects typed values).

### `'web'` caller type on ToolDefinition

`ToolCaller` in `@shardworks/tools-apparatus` is extended to include `'web'`. Tools with no `callableBy` restriction remain available to all callers (including web). Tools that explicitly restrict `callableBy` must include `'web'` to be auto-mapped. This is the only change to existing framework code.

### Custom route contributions for escape hatches

For things that don't fit the tool model (SSE streams, WebSocket connections, file uploads), plugins contribute explicit route definitions with a method, path pattern, and Hono handler function. Custom routes must be under `/api/`. If a custom route conflicts with an auto-mapped tool route, the custom route wins (with a startup warning).

### Chrome injection (server-side)

The Oculus intercepts page `index.html` responses and injects: (1) a stylesheet link in `<head>`, and (2) a navigation header after `<body>`. This means page authors get navigation and styling with zero boilerplate — just write an HTML file. Pages without `<head>` or `<body>` tags are served as-is.

### Shared stylesheet approach

The Oculus ships a static CSS file providing three layers:

1. **CSS custom properties** for theming — the standard dark palette (`--bg`, `--surface`, `--border`, `--text`, color accents)
2. **Element-type selectors** for baseline styling — `body`, `table`, `button`, `input`, `pre`, `code`, headings, links all look right with no classes
3. **Utility classes** for common patterns — `.card`, `.badge`, `.badge--success`, `.data-table`, `.btn`, `.toolbar`, `.empty-state`

The stylesheet is a static file in the package, not generated at runtime.

### Self-documenting API index

`GET /api/_tools` returns a JSON listing of all auto-mapped tools with their route, HTTP method, and parameter schemas. Page authors can hit this endpoint to discover what's available.

### Configuration

The Oculus reads `guild.json` under the `oculus` key. Only setting initially: `port` (default: `7470`).

## Out of Scope

- Authentication or access control (the dashboard is localhost-only for now)
- WebSocket support in the core (custom routes can do it, but no framework-level WebSocket machinery)
- Page-to-page communication or shared client-side state
- Server-side rendering or templating
- Asset bundling, minification, or build tooling
- A component library (just CSS — JS components can come later as patterns emerge)
- Production caching headers or CDN concerns

## Dependencies

- Hono — npm package, zero transitive deps
- The Instrumentarium (`tools`) — required for tool resolution and auto-mapping
- Kit contribution scanning — existing `consumes` + `plugin:initialized` pattern

`GET /api/_tools` returns a JSON listing of all auto-mapped tools with their route, HTTP method, and parameter schemas.

---

## Open Questions

- **Permission model for the web surface.** Currently no auth — the dashboard is localhost-only. If remote access is ever needed, what's the permission model? Deferred.
- **Client-side routing.** Pages are currently full page loads. If SPAs want hash or history routing within their page, does the Oculus need to support fallback routes per page? Probably not for MVP.

---

## Future: Component Library

The shared stylesheet covers the baseline. As patterns emerge across multiple pages (sortable tables, modal dialogs, status badges with live updates), a small JS component library (Web Components or plain helper functions) may be extracted. This is explicitly deferred — build it when we see what repeats.

message.content: is there a change we coul dmake to 'callableBy' to elminate needing 'web' as its own thing? sicne 'web' is only read by an optional plugin, it seems weird to encde that into the tools-apparatus. The real intent, i think is "mcp=anima", "cli=patron-tooling", or all. thats a bit of a mouthful, but i think cli+web are both the same thing, we just need to change the terminology

content.text:
You're absolutely right. `'cli'` and `'web'` are both patron tooling — the real distinction is *who's calling*, not *which interface*. The guild metaphor already has the perfect word for this.

**Rename `'cli'` → `'patron'`.**

```typescript
type ToolCaller = 'patron' | 'anima' | 'library'
```

- `'patron'` — any human-facing interface: CLI, web dashboard, future TUI, whatever
- `'anima'` — AI agent sessions via MCP
- `'library'` — programmatic access by engines, other apparatus code

The Oculus resolves tools with `caller: 'patron'` — same as the CLI already does (just under a new name). No new caller type, no optional plugin leaking into the core vocabulary. A tool with `callableBy: ['anima']` is MCP-only. A tool with `callableBy: ['patron']` is CLI + web + anything else the patron uses. No `callableBy` means everyone, as before.

The implementation cost is renaming `'cli'` → `'patron'` in:
- `ToolCaller` type in `tools-apparatus`
- The CLI's `resolve()` call (changes `caller: 'cli'` to `caller: 'patron'`)
- Any tools that explicitly set `callableBy: ['cli']` (if any exist)
- Tests

That's a small rename, and it makes the model more honest. Want me to update the brief and the architecture doc?

message.content: yeah, update them. remember the keep the brief brief

# The Oculus — Web Dashboard Apparatus — Commission Brief

## Problem

The guild has two interface surfaces: the `nsg` CLI and the MCP tool server (for anima sessions). Neither provides a persistent, visual overview of guild state. Previous attempts at a web dashboard produced a single monolithic apparatus and produced a broken 1000-line inline-HTML file — no extensibility, no separation of concerns, fatal JS syntax errors from escaped quotes in template literals.

What's missing is a framework-level web dashboard apparatus that any plugin can extend with its own pages and API endpoints, following the same kit contribution pattern used by tools, engines, and relays.

## Goal

A new apparatus ("The Oculus") that serves a web dashboard for the guild. Plugins contribute pages as static asset directories. Guild tools are automatically exposed as REST endpoints. The Oculus provides shared chrome (navigation) and a base stylesheet so contributed pages look cohesive with zero effort.

## Design Decisions

These decisions were made during design and should be treated as constraints, not suggestions.

### Web server: Hono

Use Hono as the HTTP framework. It has zero transitive dependencies (vs 65 for Express, 42 for Fastify, 30 for Koa), built-in TypeScript support, and a clean async API. It's a framework dependency of the Oculus package — not exposed to page authors.

### Pages as kit contributions, not widgets

The Oculus `consumes: ['pages', 'routes']`. Plugins contribute full pages — not widgets, cards, or dashboard fragments. Each page is a self-contained view. There is no cross-plugin layout coordination, no widget grid, no shared dashboard surface. If an overview/home page is wanted, the Oculus itself provides one (listing registered pages with links).

Pages may also be contributed through apparatus `supportKit`, following the same pattern as tools and engines.

### Pages are static asset directories

Each page contribution points to a directory containing static files (HTML, CSS, JS) with an `index.html` entry point. The Oculus serves these files under `/pages/{id}/`. No server-side rendering, no templating engine, no build step imposed by the Oculus. Plugins *may* use their own build steps (e.g., bundling a React app) in their publish pipeline, but that's the plugin's concern — the Oculus just serves the output directory.

Page assets are served from disk on each request (no caching). This gives hot-reload during development for free.

### Tool→REST auto-mapping with CLI-mirror naming

The Oculus automatically exposes guild tools as REST endpoints. The URL convention mirrors the CLI's hyphen-prefix grouping:

- `writ-list` → `GET /api/writ/list`
- `commission-post` → `POST /api/commission/post`
- `rig-for-writ` → `GET /api/rig/for-writ`
- `signal` → `POST /api/signal`

This is a mechanical transform — first hyphen splits prefix from rest, prepend `/api/`. No RESTful resource inference, no pluralization, no ambiguity.

HTTP method is inferred from the tool's `permission` level: `read` (or no permission) → GET, `write`/`admin` → POST, `delete` → DELETE.

For GET endpoints, query string parameters need the same string→number/boolean coercion the CLI applies (all query params arrive as strings; the tool's Zod schema expects typed values).

### `'web'` caller type on ToolDefinition

`ToolCaller` in `@shardworks/tools-apparatus` is extended to include `'web'`. Tools with no `callableBy` restriction remain available to all callers (including web). Tools that explicitly restrict `callableBy` must include `'web'` to be auto-mapped. This is the only change to existing framework code.

### Custom route contributions for escape hatches

For things that don't fit the tool model (SSE streams, WebSocket connections, file uploads), plugins contribute explicit route definitions with a method, path pattern, and Hono handler function. Custom routes must be under `/api/`. If a custom route conflicts with an auto-mapped tool route, the custom route wins (with a startup warning).

### Chrome injection (server-side)

The Oculus intercepts page `index.html` responses and injects: (1) a stylesheet link in `<head>`, and (2) a navigation header after `<body>`. This means page authors get navigation and styling with zero boilerplate — just write an HTML file. Pages without `<head>` or `<body>` tags are served as-is.

### Shared stylesheet approach

The Oculus ships a static CSS file providing three layers:

1. **CSS custom properties** for theming — the standard dark palette (`--bg`, `--surface`, `--border`, `--text`, color accents)
2. **Element-type selectors** for baseline styling — `body`, `table`, `button`, `input`, `pre`, `code`, headings, links all look right with no classes
3. **Utility classes** for common patterns — `.card`, `.badge`, `.badge--success`, `.data-table`, `.btn`, `.toolbar`, `.empty-state`

The stylesheet is a static file in the package, not generated at runtime.

### Self-documenting API index

`GET /api/_tools` returns a JSON listing of all auto-mapped tools with their route, HTTP method, and parameter schemas. Page authors can hit this endpoint to discover what's available.

### Configuration

The Oculus reads `guild.json` under the `oculus` key. Only setting initially: `port` (default: `7470`).

## Out of Scope

- Authentication or access control (the dashboard is localhost-only for now)
- WebSocket support in the core (custom routes can do it, but no framework-level WebSocket machinery)
- Page-to-page communication or shared client-side state
- Server-side rendering or templating
- Asset bundling, minification, or build tooling
- A component library (just CSS — JS components can come later as patterns emerge)
- Production caching headers or CDN concerns

## Dependencies

- Hono — npm package, zero transitive deps
- The Instrumentarium (`tools`) — required for tool resolution and auto-mapping
- Kit contribution scanning — existing `consumes` + `plugin:initialized` pattern

# The Oculus — API Contract

Status: **Placeholder — Pre-Implementation**

Package: `@shardworks/oculus-apparatus` · Plugin id: `oculus`

> **⚠️ Pre-implementation.** This document captures the design decisions for the Oculus. It will be expanded into a full API contract during implementation.

---

## Purpose

The Oculus is the guild's web dashboard. It serves a browser-based interface for observing and interacting with guild state, providing a persistent visual overview that the CLI and MCP tool server do not offer.

The Oculus is an **aggregator, not a producer** — it does not own any guild data. It consumes `pages` and `routes` from kit contributions, auto-maps installed guild tools as REST endpoints, and serves a unified multi-page UI with shared chrome and styling. Any plugin can extend the dashboard by contributing a page.

The Oculus does **not** handle authentication, authorization beyond tool-level permissions, server-side rendering, or asset bundling.

---

## Dependencies

```
requires: ['tools']      — needs the Instrumentarium for tool resolution and auto-mapping
consumes: ['pages', 'routes']
```

- **The Instrumentarium** — the Oculus resolves tools callable by `'web'` and auto-maps them as REST endpoints.

---

## Kit Interface

Kits and apparatus contribute pages and custom API routes to the Oculus. A page is a directory of static assets (HTML, CSS, JS) with an `index.html` entry point. Custom routes handle patterns that don't fit the tool model (SSE streams, WebSocket connections, etc.).

```typescript
// Example: an apparatus contributing a page via supportKit
export default {
  apparatus: {
    // ...
    supportKit: {
      pages: [{
        id: 'writs',
        title: 'Writs',
        assetsDir: path.join(__dirname, '../assets/writs'),
      }],
      routes: [{
        method: 'GET',
        path: '/api/session/:id/stream',
        handler: sseHandler,
      }],
    },
  },
} satisfies Plugin
```

---

## Support Kit

The Oculus has no support kit — it does not contribute tools, engines, or relays.

---

## `OculusApi` Interface (`provides`)

```typescript
interface OculusApi {
  /** The port the server is listening on. */
  port(): number
  /** The full base URL (e.g. http://localhost:7470). */
  url(): string
  /** List of registered pages with id, title, and URL. */
  pages(): PageInfo[]
}
```

---

## Configuration

```json
{
  "oculus": {
    "port": 7470
  }
}
```

**`port`** — the HTTP port for the dashboard server. Default: `7470`.

---

## Key Design Decisions

These decisions were made during patron design review and are **authoritative constraints**, not suggestions.

### Web Server: Hono

The Oculus uses [Hono](https://hono.dev/) as its HTTP framework. Hono has zero transitive dependencies, built-in TypeScript, and a clean async API. It was selected over Express (65 transitive deps), Fastify (42 deps, competing plugin model), and Koa (30 deps + separate router package). Hono is a framework dependency of the Oculus package — not exposed to page authors.

### Pages, Not Widgets

The Oculus provides full-page contributions only — no widgets, cards, or dashboard fragments. Each page is a self-contained view. There is no cross-plugin layout coordination, no widget grid, no shared dashboard surface. The Oculus provides a home page that lists all registered pages with links.

### Static Asset Directories

Each page contribution points to a directory of static files with an `index.html` entry point. The Oculus serves these files under `/pages/{id}/`. No server-side rendering, no templating, no build step imposed by the framework. Plugins may use their own build steps in their publish pipelines — the Oculus just serves the output directory.

Page assets are served from disk on each request (no caching) for hot-reload during development.

### Tool→REST Auto-Mapping with CLI-Mirror Naming

Guild tools are automatically exposed as REST endpoints. The URL convention mirrors the CLI's hyphen-prefix grouping — a mechanical transform with no ambiguity:

```
writ-list       → GET  /api/writ/list
commission-post → POST /api/commission/post
rig-for-writ    → GET  /api/rig/for-writ
signal          → POST /api/signal
```

First hyphen splits prefix from rest, prepend `/api/`. No RESTful resource inference, no pluralization.

HTTP method is inferred from the tool's `permission` level: `read` (or no permission) → GET, `write`/`admin` → POST, `delete` → DELETE.

For GET endpoints, query string parameters require string→number/boolean coercion (same pattern as the CLI's `coerceCliOpts`).

### `'web'` Caller Type

`ToolCaller` in `@shardworks/tools-apparatus` is extended to include `'web'`: `'cli' | 'anima' | 'library' | 'web'`. Tools with no `callableBy` restriction remain available to all callers including web. Tools that explicitly restrict `callableBy` must include `'web'` to be auto-mapped. This is the only change to existing framework code outside the Oculus package.

### Custom Routes for Escape Hatches

For patterns that don't fit the tool model (SSE streams, WebSocket, long-polling), plugins contribute explicit route definitions with a method, path, and Hono handler. Custom routes must be under `/api/`. If a custom route conflicts with an auto-mapped tool route, the custom route wins with a startup warning.

### Chrome Injection (Server-Side)

The Oculus intercepts page `index.html` responses and injects: (1) a stylesheet link in `<head>`, and (2) a navigation header after `<body>`. Page authors get navigation and styling with zero boilerplate. Pages without recognizable `<head>` or `<body>` tags are served as-is.

### Shared Stylesheet: Three Layers

The Oculus ships a static CSS file at `/oculus/styles.css` providing:

1. **CSS custom properties** for theming — `--bg`, `--surface`, `--border`, `--text`, color accents
2. **Element-type selectors** for baseline — `body`, `table`, `button`, `input`, `pre`, `code`, headings, links all styled with no classes needed
3. **Utility classes** for common patterns — `.card`, `.badge`, `.badge--success`, `.data-table`, `.btn`, `.toolbar`, `.empty-state`

### Self-Documenting API Index

`GET /api/_tools` returns a JSON listing of all auto-mapped tools with their route, HTTP method, and parameter schemas.

---

## Open Questions

- **Permission model for the web surface.** Currently no auth — the dashboard is localhost-only. If remote access is ever needed, what's the permission model? Deferred.
- **Client-side routing.** Pages are currently full page loads. If SPAs want hash or history routing within their page, does the Oculus need to support fallback routes per page? Probably not for MVP.

---

## Future: Component Library

The shared stylesheet covers the baseline. As patterns emerge across multiple pages (sortable tables, modal dialogs, status badges with live updates), a small JS component library (Web Components or plain helper functions) may be extracted. This is explicitly deferred — build it when we see what repeats.

-### `'web'` Caller Type
+### Rename `'cli'` Caller Type to `'patron'`

-`ToolCaller` in `@shardworks/tools-apparatus` is extended to include `'web'`: `'cli' | 'anima' | 'library' | 'web'`. Tools with no `callableBy` restriction remain available to all callers including web. Tools that explicitly restrict `callableBy` must include `'web'` to be auto-mapped. This is the only change to existing framework code outside the Oculus package.

+`ToolCaller` in `@shardworks/tools-apparatus` is renamed from `'cli' | 'anima' | 'library'` to `'patron' | 'anima' | 'library'`. The `'patron'` caller covers all human-facing interfaces — CLI, web dashboard, any future patron tooling. The Oculus resolves tools with `caller: 'patron'`, same as the CLI. No new caller type is introduced. Tools with no `callableBy` restriction remain available to all callers. Tools that explicitly restrict `callableBy` must include `'patron'` to be auto-mapped.

# The Oculus — Web Dashboard Apparatus — Commission Brief

## Problem

The guild has two interface surfaces: the `nsg` CLI and the MCP tool server (for anima sessions). Neither provides a persistent, visual overview of guild state. Previous attempts at a web dashboard produced a single monolithic apparatus and produced a broken 1000-line inline-HTML file — no extensibility, no separation of concerns, fatal JS syntax errors from escaped quotes in template literals.

What's missing is a framework-level web dashboard apparatus that any plugin can extend with its own pages and API endpoints, following the same kit contribution pattern used by tools, engines, and relays.

## Goal

A new apparatus ("The Oculus") that serves a web dashboard for the guild. Plugins contribute pages as static asset directories. Guild tools are automatically exposed as REST endpoints. The Oculus provides shared chrome (navigation) and a base stylesheet so contributed pages look cohesive with zero effort.

## Design Decisions

These decisions were made during design and should be treated as constraints, not suggestions.

### Web server: Hono

Use Hono as the HTTP framework. It has zero transitive dependencies (vs 65 for Express, 42 for Fastify, 30 for Koa), built-in TypeScript support, and a clean async API. It's a framework dependency of the Oculus package — not exposed to page authors.

### Pages as kit contributions, not widgets

The Oculus `consumes: ['pages', 'routes']`. Plugins contribute full pages — not widgets, cards, or dashboard fragments. Each page is a self-contained view. There is no cross-plugin layout coordination, no widget grid, no shared dashboard surface. If an overview/home page is wanted, the Oculus itself provides one (listing registered pages with links).

Pages may also be contributed through apparatus `supportKit`, following the same pattern as tools and engines.

### Pages are static asset directories

Each page contribution points to a directory containing static files (HTML, CSS, JS) with an `index.html` entry point. The Oculus serves these files under `/pages/{id}/`. No server-side rendering, no templating engine, no build step imposed by the Oculus. Plugins *may* use their own build steps (e.g., bundling a React app) in their publish pipeline, but that's the plugin's concern — the Oculus just serves the output directory.

Page assets are served from disk on each request (no caching). This gives hot-reload during development for free.

### Tool→REST auto-mapping with CLI-mirror naming

The Oculus automatically exposes guild tools as REST endpoints. The URL convention mirrors the CLI's hyphen-prefix grouping:

- `writ-list` → `GET /api/writ/list`
- `commission-post` → `POST /api/commission/post`
- `rig-for-writ` → `GET /api/rig/for-writ`
- `signal` → `POST /api/signal`

This is a mechanical transform — first hyphen splits prefix from rest, prepend `/api/`. No RESTful resource inference, no pluralization, no ambiguity.

HTTP method is inferred from the tool's `permission` level: `read` (or no permission) → GET, `write`/`admin` → POST, `delete` → DELETE.

For GET endpoints, query string parameters need the same string→number/boolean coercion the CLI applies (all query params arrive as strings; the tool's Zod schema expects typed values).

### Rename `'cli'` caller type to `'patron'`

`ToolCaller` in `@shardworks/tools-apparatus` is renamed from `'cli' | 'anima' | 'library'` to `'patron' | 'anima' | 'library'`. The `'patron'` caller covers all human-facing interfaces — CLI, web dashboard, any future patron tooling. The Oculus resolves tools with `caller: 'patron'`, same as the CLI. No new caller type needed.

### Custom route contributions for escape hatches

For things that don't fit the tool model (SSE streams, WebSocket connections, file uploads), plugins contribute explicit route definitions with a method, path pattern, and Hono handler function. Custom routes must be under `/api/`. If a custom route conflicts with an auto-mapped tool route, the custom route wins (with a startup warning).

### Chrome injection (server-side)

The Oculus intercepts page `index.html` responses and injects: (1) a stylesheet link in `<head>`, and (2) a navigation header after `<body>`. This means page authors get navigation and styling with zero boilerplate — just write an HTML file. Pages without `<head>` or `<body>` tags are served as-is.

### Shared stylesheet approach

The Oculus ships a static CSS file providing three layers:

1. **CSS custom properties** for theming — the standard dark palette (`--bg`, `--surface`, `--border`, `--text`, color accents)
2. **Element-type selectors** for baseline styling — `body`, `table`, `button`, `input`, `pre`, `code`, headings, links all look right with no classes
3. **Utility classes** for common patterns — `.card`, `.badge`, `.badge--success`, `.data-table`, `.btn`, `.toolbar`, `.empty-state`

The stylesheet is a static file in the package, not generated at runtime.

### Self-documenting API index

`GET /api/_tools` returns a JSON listing of all auto-mapped tools with their route, HTTP method, and parameter schemas. Page authors can hit this endpoint to discover what's available.

### Configuration

The Oculus reads `guild.json` under the `oculus` key. Only setting initially: `port` (default: `7470`).

## Out of Scope

- Authentication or access control (the dashboard is localhost-only for now)
- WebSocket support in the core (custom routes can do it, but no framework-level WebSocket machinery)
- Page-to-page communication or shared client-side state
- Server-side rendering or templating
- Asset bundling, minification, or build tooling
- A component library (just CSS — JS components can come later as patterns emerge)
- Production caching headers or CDN concerns

## Dependencies

- Hono — npm package, zero transitive deps
- The Instrumentarium (`tools`) — required for tool resolution and auto-mapping
- Kit contribution scanning — existing `consumes` + `plugin:initialized` pattern

+Dark, monospace, terminal-aesthetic. The plan-workshop (`bin/plan-review.ts` in the sanctum, not the framework) is the reference for the visual language. Key characteristics:
+- **Palette** — Tokyo Night-inspired dark theme. Deep navy backgrounds, muted text, cyan/green/magenta/yellow accents.
+- **Typography** — monospace font stack (`"SF Mono", "Fira Code", "JetBrains Mono", monospace`), 13px base, 1.6 line-height.
+- **Surfaces** — layered backgrounds (`--bg` → `--surface` → `--surface2`) with subtle 1px borders. Rounded corners (6-8px).
+- **Controls** — buttons are solid accent colors with dark text, not outlined. Inputs have dark backgrounds with border highlights on focus.
+- **Status indication** — colored badges with translucent tinted backgrounds (e.g., `rgba(158,206,106,0.15)` with `--green` text). Pulsing animation for active/running states.
+- **Density** — compact but not cramped. 13px font, 8-16px padding, 8px gaps. Information-dense like a terminal, not spacious like a marketing site.

+:root {
+  --text: #c0caf5; --text-dim: #565f89; --text-bright: #e0e6ff;
+  --green: #9ece6a; --red: #f7768e; --yellow: #e0af68; --cyan: #7dcfff;
+  --magenta: #bb9af7; --blue: #7aa2f7;

content.text:
🐒 Off it goes!

One thing to flag — the architecture doc (`docs/architecture/apparatus/oculus.md`) is sitting uncommitted in the nexus repo because of that diverged branch with merge conflicts. The anima will need that doc in place as an authoritative reference when it starts working. We should sort out that branch conflict before dispatching the commission.

Base directory for this skill: /workspace/nexus-mk2/.claude/skills/wrap-up

# Wrap-Up: Complete Work and Generate Session Summary

## Generate Summary

Generate a structured summary of the current session. This summary serves two audiences:

1. **The next Coco session** — as startup context to resume work
2. **The Scribe** — as input for session notes and published material

<expected-format>
# Session Summary

## What we did

- Bulleted list of concrete actions taken (files created/modified, decisions made, commits pushed). Reference specific files and commit hashes.

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- What was decided, including explicit "we chose NOT to" decisions. Brief rationale for each.

## Next steps & open questions

- Concrete actions for the next session or for agents to pick up.
- Open questions which were raised but not answered

### Notable moments

Capture quotes, insights, or exchanges that stand out — either for system design or for the research/experiment narrative.

For each notable moment:
- Include **direct quotes from Sean** when available
- Flag **why it's notable** (e.g., "shifted the project's core abstraction", "surfaced a tension between X and Y", "revealed an assumption about how agents should work")
- Prioritize moments where the human changed direction, challenged an assumption, or articulated something new about the project's purpose

### Experiment observations

Flag anything relevant to active experiments. Omit this section if nothing applies.

- **X008 (Patron's Hands):** If Coco did implementation work this session, note: what task, how it was directed (Sean asked vs. Coco offered), scope (files/lines), and whether a commission was considered as an alternative.
- **X007 (First Contact):** If an autonomous commission was dispatched or discussed, note the context.
<expected-format>

## Complete Work

After wrapping up, **ALWAYS**:

- Commit any changes you have made, if any
- Push main to the remote

## Reminders

Check for things Sean should be nudged about:

- **Ethnographer interview:** Check the most recent interview file in `experiments/ethnography/interviews/` (files named `YYYY-MM-DDTHHMMSS.md`). If the most recent one is more than a day old — or if none exist yet — remind Sean to run an ethnographer session. Keep it light: *"It's been a couple days since your last ethnographer interview — might be a good time to check in."*

## Guidelines

- Keep the whole summary under ~500 words. Brevity is a feature.
- Do not editorialize beyond the "why it's notable" flags.
- Present the summary directly as part of the conversation
- Write the summary to `.scratch/recent-sessions/<timestamp>.md`, where `<timestamp>` is the current date and time formatted as `YYYY-MM-DDTHHMMSS.md` (use the actual current time — don't round to the nearest hour). Each session gets its own file — never overwrite a previous one.
- **Also write a copy** to `experiments/ethnography/session-notes/new/<timestamp>.md`. This copy is for the ethnographer and must NOT be auto-deleted — it accumulates until the ethnographer reviews it.
- After writing, check `.scratch/recent-sessions/`: if there are more than 4 files, delete the oldest ones to keep only the 4 most recent. Do NOT trim the `session-notes/new/` copy.
- Commit all `recent-sessions`, `session-notes`, and `experiments/data/transcripts/` changes to git and push those changes. The transcripts directory may contain files from concurrent sessions — commit whatever is there.
- In addition to writing the summary, share it in the chat for review and inclusion in session transcripts

# Session Summary — 2026-04-07T193000

## What we did

- **Designed The Oculus** — a new web dashboard apparatus for the Nexus framework. Three rounds of interactive design discussion covering plugin model, web framework, REST API shape, styling approach, and naming.
- **Created commission brief** at `.scratch/oculus-brief.md` — distilled design decisions into a planner-ready brief. Sean edited to trim sanctum-specific references from the Problem section.

# Coco Log — Standing Research Instrument
#
# One entry per unit of work handled directly by Coco (the interactive
# collaborator) rather than dispatched as an autonomous commission.
# Parallel to the commission log — together they capture the full
# picture of how work is routed in the system.
#
# An entry is created for each commit (or tight cluster of commits
# toward a single goal) that Coco produces during a session.
#
# Fields:
#   session        — Coco's Claude session ID (join key to archived transcripts)
#   date           — date the work was done (YYYY-MM-DD)
#   item           — short description of the work (one line)
#   commissionable — whether this work could have been an autonomous commission
#   justification  — why it can't be commissioned (required when commissionable is false)
#
# Justifications (required when commissionable: false):
#   interactive     — required real-time iteration with Sean
#   sanctum         — sanctum/experiment/meta work outside the commission boundary
#   decisional      — output was a decision, not an artifact
